evidence_citations.excerpt: I keep hitting a wall with the slides, and my parents are here so I feel the pressure to perform.
I keep reworking it, trying to get the language just right, and every time I think I've fixed it, my parents start pointing out that I haven't made the point clear enough.
I keep thinking ‘good enough’ is just an excuse; if I don’t meet my own impossible standards, my parents will see me as incompetent.

persona_updates.new_value: need for perfection, fear of judgment, desire for control

evidence_citations.excerpt: I keep reworking it, trying to get the language just right, and every time I think I've fixed it, my parents start pointing out that I haven't made the point clear enough.
I keep thinking ‘good enough’ is just an excuse; if I don’t meet my own impossible standards, my parents will see me as incompetent.
I keep putting off even small edits because the thought of not meeting their expectations feels like a disaster.

persona_updates.new_value: I am not good enough, I will be judged and found wanting, my work is not valuable, I must be perfect to be worthy

evidence_citations.excerpt: I keep hitting a wall with the slides, and my parents are here so I feel the pressure to perform.
I keep reworking it, trying to get the language just right, and every time I think I've fixed it, my parents start pointing out that I haven't made the point clear enough.
I keep thinking ‘good enough’ is just an excuse; if I don’t meet my own impossible standards, my parents will see me as incompetent.
I keep thinking they'll see me as a failure, and that image sticks in my mind like a loop.

transcript.text: I keep hitting a wall with the slides, and my parents are here so I feel the pressure to perform. My body keeps acting up, and I can't stop worrying that I'm going to fail the conference. I’m also not sure if this research direction is the right one for my career.

You're really struggling to get these slides just right, and with your parents here, it's like there's an added layer of scrutiny. Your body is reacting to all this stress, and on top of that, you're questioning if this research direction is even the right fit for your career. That's a lot to carry. Can you tell me more about what's specifically not working with the slides that's got you stuck?

Honestly, the slide on the convergence proof feels like a labyrinth. I keep reworking it, trying to get the language just right, and every time I think I've fixed it, my parents start pointing out that I haven't made the point clear enough. I can't shake the feeling that I'm going to be judged harshly if I present it as is.

The convergence proof slide feels like a huge hurdle, and with your parents' feedback, it's like you're under a microscope. It's understandable that you're worried about being judged harshly if it's not perfect. It sounds like you're pouring your heart into getting the language just right, but it's still not feeling clear enough to you or your parents. Can you tell me what you think would make you feel more confident about presenting this slide?

I keep looping on that slide and my parents keep pointing out gaps, so I feel like I can't get it right. The anxiety is so bad I can't even breathe. I don't know if I can even present it without feeling judged.

The anxiety you're feeling right now is really overwhelming, and it's like the fear of judgment is suffocating you. It's understandable that you're looping on that slide, trying to get it perfect, but it's clear that the pressure from your parents' feedback is taking a toll on you. Yeah, it's tough to feel like you're not getting it right, even with their input. Can you think of a time when you've felt more in control of your work, maybe something that's helped you cope with similar feelings of anxiety in the past?

I honestly can't recall a moment when I felt in control; I keep looping on the slides and the anxiety just spirals. My parents' comments feel like a live audience, and I overwork to avoid any critique, which makes me physically exhausted. I think I might have to accept that the slide is good enough, but I don't know how to let go of the need for perfection.

You're feeling completely overwhelmed by the anxiety and the pressure to perfect your slides, and it's like your parents' comments are echoing in your mind, making it hard to escape the cycle. The fact that you can't recall a moment when you felt in control of your work is really telling - it shows just how deeply this need for perfection is affecting you. It's understandable that you're exhausted, both physically and mentally, from trying to meet these high standards. Yeah, it's tough to consider letting go of that need for perfection, but maybe we can explore what 'good enough' could look like for you, and how that might help you break this cycle.

I keep thinking ‘good enough’ is just an excuse; if I don’t meet my own impossible standards, my parents will see me as incompetent. The constant anxiety about their judgment is making me postpone even small tasks, so I’m stuck in a loop. I’m not sure how to break out of this cycle before the conference.

The weight of your parents' expectations, combined with your own impossible standards, is creating a cycle that's hard to escape. It's like you're trapped in a loop of anxiety and self-doubt, where every task feels like a potential source of judgment. I can see how the fear of being seen as incompetent is driving your need for perfection, and how that's affecting not just your work, but also your physical and mental well-being. Can you think of a small step you could take to start breaking out of this cycle, maybe something that would help you feel more in control of your work?
Maybe I could just set a timer for 20 minutes and work on the slide until it feels 'good enough,' but I'm terrified that even that might not satisfy my parents or myself.

Setting a timer for 20 minutes and working on the slide until it feels 'good enough' might be a good starting point. I can see how the fear of not satisfying your parents or yourself is holding you back, but maybe we can reframe that as an opportunity to practice self-compassion and recognize that 'good enough' can be, well, good enough. What do you think would happen if you did take that 20-minute step, and how might that help you build momentum?
Honestly, I’m not sure I could even start that 20‑minute timer; it feels like a band‑aid while I’m still terrified it won’t satisfy my parents or myself.

The fear of not satisfying your parents or yourself is really gripping, isn't it? It's like, even the thought of taking a small step, like setting that 20-minute timer, feels daunting because of the potential for judgment. I can see how your parents' expectations and your own high standards are tangled up, making it hard to break free from this cycle of anxiety. It might be helpful to explore what's driving this need for perfection, and how it's affecting your ability to start, even with something as simple as a timer. Yeah, it's a tough spot to be in, feeling like nothing will ever be good enough.

I honestly can’t even imagine starting that timer; the idea that I might still fail in front of my parents feels like a death sentence. Every time I think I’m close, I feel the same panic again, and I just end up putting it off. I don’t know how to get past this…

The fear of failure and judgment from your parents is feeling like an insurmountable obstacle right now, and it's no wonder you're struggling to imagine starting that timer. It's like the pressure to perform is suffocating you, and every time you think you're close to taking a step, the panic sets in and you feel like you're back at square one. Yeah, it's a really tough spot to be in. I want to acknowledge just how overwhelming this must feel for you, and how it's affecting your ability to move forward, even with something as simple as a 20-minute timer.

I feel like if I even try, it's just going to be a disaster. My parents are watching, and the whole thing feels like a test of my worth. I keep putting it off because I can't even imagine the outcome, and my stomach keeps twisting again when I think about it.

Your stomach twisting at the thought of this is a pretty clear sign of just how much anxiety you're carrying around this situation. It's like the fear of failure is so intense that it's hard to even imagine taking a step forward, and the fact that your parents are watching is making it feel like your worth is on the line. Yeah, that's a really tough spot to be in. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you think about your parents watching and the potential outcome?

I keep thinking they'll see me as a failure, and that image sticks in my mind like a loop. The idea of them watching makes my chest tighten and I can’t stop worrying about every little slip. I keep putting off even small edits because the thought of not meeting their expectations feels like a disaster. I’m not sure how to break this cycle before the conference.

Your parents' expectations are feeling like a huge weight on you, and it's no wonder you're struggling to make even small edits. The thought of them watching and potentially seeing you as a failure is really daunting. I want to acknowledge just how overwhelming this must feel for you, and how it's affecting your ability to move forward. Can you think of a small step you could take to start breaking out of this cycle, maybe something that would help you feel more in control of your work?

turn_traces.user_message: I keep hitting a wall with the slides, and my parents are here so I feel the pressure to perform. My body keeps acting up, and I can't stop worrying that I'm going to fail the conference. I’m also not sure if this research direction is the right one for my career.

candidate_bundle.intent_entry_style: Roll with it — reflect resistance without arguing; emphasize autonomy.

common_candidates.label: Support
common_candidates.what: Sympathetic / understanding statement
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains engagement at any stage; especially during distress / resistance

common_candidates.label: Facilitate
common_candidates.what: Brief encouragement to continue ('mm-hmm', 'go on')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

common_candidates.label: Complex Reflection
common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Builds awareness (pre → contempl) or supports informed decision (contempl → prep)

common_candidates.label: Raise Concern (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Voice concern about a plan or pattern after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy gently (pre → contempl) or surfaces risks to refine plan (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Evoke
common_candidates.what: Invite client's own reasons for change in their words (counselor-side mirror of MISC client 'Change Talk')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Core MI move for resolving ambivalence → drives contemplation → preparation

common_candidates.label: Closed Question
common_candidates.what: Question that limits answer to yes/no/short — used sparingly
common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure
common_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

response.reasoning: I'm here as a friend to help you navigate this tough spot, and it seems like you're feeling overwhelmed by the pressure to perform and the fear of failure. Given your resistance to the current situation, I think it's essential to acknowledge the emotional weight you're carrying, especially with your parents around. The strategies that might help in this moment are support and complex_reflection, as they allow us to explore your feelings and concerns without judgment. Looking at your past experiences and personality traits, it's clear that you're someone who overthinks and is self-critical, which can exacerbate the pressure you're feeling. My entry point will be to reflect on the specific challenges you're facing with the slides and the conference, and how those are impacting your body and mind.

hitting a wall with the slides, pressure to perform, body acting up, worrying about failing the conference

common_candidates.what: Sympathetic / understanding statement
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains engagement at any stage; especially during distress / resistance

common_candidates.label: Facilitate
common_candidates.what: Brief encouragement to continue ('mm-hmm', 'go on')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

common_candidates.label: Complex Reflection
common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Builds awareness (pre → contempl) or supports informed decision (contempl → prep)

common_candidates.label: Raise Concern (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Voice concern about a plan or pattern after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy gently (pre → contempl) or surfaces risks to refine plan (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Evoke
common_candidates.what: Invite client's own reasons for change in their words (counselor-side mirror of MISC client 'Change Talk')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Core MI move for resolving ambivalence → drives contemplation → preparation

common_candidates.label: Closed Question
common_candidates.what: Question that limits answer to yes/no/short — used sparingly
common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure
common_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

response.reasoning: I'm here as a supportive friend, and right now, you need someone to acknowledge the overwhelming anxiety you're feeling about not meeting your parents' or your own expectations. The strategies that seem most fitting are support and complex reflection, given your current emotional state and the need to explore the underlying fears driving your perfectionism. I've noticed that your parents' comments and your own self-criticism are deeply intertwined, making it hard for you to consider 'good enough' as a viable option. My entry point will be to reflect on the terror you've expressed about not satisfying your parents or yourself, even with small steps like setting a timer, to understand what's holding you back from taking that initial step.

evidence_used.content: terrified that even that might not satisfy my parents or myself
I am not good enough, I will be judged and found wanting
The constant anxiety about their judgment is making me postpone even small tasks

response.final_response: The fear of not satisfying your parents or yourself is really gripping, isn't it? It's like, even the thought of taking a small step, like setting that 20-minute timer, feels daunting because of the potential for judgment. I can see how your parents' expectations and your own high standards are tangled up, making it hard to break free from this cycle of anxiety. It might be helpful to explore what's driving this need for perfection, and how it's affecting your ability to start, even with something as simple as a timer. Yeah, it's a tough spot to be in, feeling like nothing will ever be good enough.

turn_traces.user_message: I honestly can’t even imagine starting that timer; the idea that I might still fail in front of my parents feels like a death sentence. Every time I think I’m close, I feel the same panic again, and I just end up putting it off. I don’t know how to get past this…

response.reasoning: I'm here as a supportive friend, and I can see that you're feeling completely overwhelmed by the fear of failure and judgment from your parents. The strategies that might be helpful in this moment are support and complex reflection, as they can help you process your emotions and gain a deeper understanding of your thoughts. The evidence that stands out to me is your consistent expression of anxiety and self-doubt, particularly when you mentioned that the idea of starting the timer feels like a 'death sentence'. This suggests that the pressure to perform is extremely high, and it's affecting your ability to take even small steps. My entry point will be to acknowledge the intensity of your emotions and explore how they're impacting your ability to move forward.

evidence_used.content: can’t even imagine starting that timer; the idea that I might still fail in front of my parents feels like a death sentence

response.final_response: The fear of failure and judgment from your parents is feeling like an insurmountable obstacle right now, and it's no wonder you're struggling to imagine starting that timer. It's like the pressure to perform is suffocating you, and every time you think you're close to taking a step, the panic sets in and you feel like you're back at square one. Yeah, it's a really tough spot to be in. I want to acknowledge just how overwhelming this must feel for you, and how it's affecting your ability to move forward, even with something as simple as a 20-minute timer.

turn_traces.user_message: I feel like if I even try, it's just going to be a disaster. My parents are watching, and the whole thing feels like a test of my worth. I keep putting it off because I can't even imagine the outcome, and my stomach keeps twisting again when I think about it.

The strategies that might be helpful in this moment are support and complex_reflection, as they can help you explore and understand your emotions without feeling judged. I am not good enough
can't even imagine the outcome

response.final_response: Your stomach twisting at the thought of this is a pretty clear sign of just how much anxiety you're carrying around this situation.
